narrative:
Here’s your **First Warn Forecast** for Wednesday, written in your style with strong flow, analysis, and clean paragraphs:

---

First Warn Forecast
Wednesday, May 6, 2026
First Warn Weather Headlines

* Cooler and drier weather settles in through Friday
* Chilly nights with a *very low* chance of patchy frost in sheltered areas
* A warming trend begins late week, peaking Saturday
* Multiple chances for showers and thunderstorms return Friday night through Sunday
* Severe weather is **not expected at this time**

Today – Wednesday

If you stepped outside this morning and thought, “This doesn’t feel like May,” you’re not wrong.

We’re firmly on the backside of yesterday’s cold front, and the atmosphere has transitioned into a classic post-frontal pattern. Cold air advection continues to filter into the region on a light northerly flow, while lingering low-level moisture trapped beneath a shallow inversion keeps clouds hanging around longer than we’d prefer.

That combination—cool air moving in and clouds overhead—is going to hold temperatures in check today. Highs will struggle to reach the upper 50s to near 60 degrees across much of the area. For early May, that’s a noticeable step below average, and it’s the kind of cool that sticks with you through the day.

From a precipitation standpoint, the story is quiet. The post-frontal air mass is relatively dry, and more importantly, we lack any meaningful lift or forcing mechanisms. Without that vertical motion, the atmosphere simply doesn’t have what it needs to produce rain. So while clouds may linger at times, conditions remain dry.

Tonight into Thursday

As we head into tonight, things begin to settle down a bit more. Clouds will gradually thin, winds will ease, and the atmosphere will start to decouple. That opens the door for radiational cooling, allowing temperatures to fall off fairly efficiently overnight.

Lows will drop into the upper 30s to low 40s, and while that might raise eyebrows this time of year, widespread frost concerns remain low. The setup isn’t perfect—winds won’t go completely calm everywhere, and some residual cloud cover may linger. That said, in the typical colder spots—valleys and sheltered rural areas—you could see a touch of patchy frost. It’s not a widespread issue, but it’s something to keep in mind if you’re in those prone locations.

By Thursday, the pattern starts to relax. Cold air advection weakens, skies trend mostly clear, and with more sunshine, temperatures rebound into the 60s. Still a bit below normal, but noticeably more comfortable than today.


Looking Ahead – Late Week into the Weekend

The quiet stretch doesn’t last forever.

By Friday, the pattern begins to shift once again as southwesterly flow returns and opens the door for Gulf moisture to creep back northward. Embedded within that flow will be a series of mid-level disturbances, and those will bring our next chances for showers and thunderstorms starting Friday night and continuing at times through the weekend.

From a meteorological standpoint, this is an interesting setup—but not a threatening one.

Even though wind shear values may be respectable, instability remains limited. Most guidance keeps surface-based CAPE in the 400 to 500 J/kg range, which is on the lower end for sustaining strong convection. On top of that, the atmospheric profile is characterized by what we call “long and skinny CAPE.” In simple terms, there’s not enough robust buoyancy through the depth of the atmosphere to support strong, persistent updrafts.

Add in fairly unidirectional wind profiles, and the ingredients just don’t align for organized severe weather. So while we’ll see showers and thunderstorms at times, **the risk for severe storms remains very low right now**.

Weekend Outlook

Temperatures will respond to the returning southerly flow.

We begin a gradual warm-up Friday, with the peak expected on Saturday when highs climb into the 70s and even low 80s in spots. That will be the warmest day of the stretch and a nice rebound from the midweek cool-down.

Another, more pronounced system arrives Sunday, bringing a stronger cold front through the region. This will trigger another round of showers and thunderstorms, especially ahead of the front. Even with this more organized system, a notable capping inversion is expected to limit storm intensity, keeping severe weather potential low.

Behind that front, cooler air returns early next week, before another warming trend sets in by midweek.

Bottom Line

Cool, quiet, and dry through the end of the workweek, with a noticeable chill at night. The weekend brings a return to warmer temperatures along with periodic chances for rain, but at this point, **nothing suggests a severe weather threat**.

Sometimes spring likes to keep us guessing—and this week is a good reminder of that.

First Warn Weather – First. Accurate. Trusted.
